revision.comment: Programmatically generating so that if this gets imported to wikipedia as a draft, the bot will attribute it to Immanuelle and send them g6 warnings
revision.text: #redirect[[en:Naorai]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

'''直会'''（なおらい）とは[[神社]]に於ける[[神事]]の内、神事が終了した後に神事に参加したもの一同で[[神饌]]を食する行事である。
神饌が食べられるものであるという証でもある。
神事によっては、神と一緒に食事をすると言う物もあるが、決められた神饌であることが多く、'''直会'''とは異なる神事である。
また、神饌には季節の野菜、魚介類なども供えられるのでそれを調理した物が出される場合が多い。この為、神社によっては'''直会'''での料理は郷土料理と同一の場合がある。
神社から餅などを頂く場合、'''直らう'''と言う場合がある。これも似た意味である。
{{stub}} [[category:神道|なおらい]]

'''直会'''（なおらい）とは[[神社]]に於ける[[神事]]の内、神事が終了した後に神事に参加したもの一同で[[神饌]]を食する行事である。
神饌が食べられるものであるという証でもある。
神事によっては、神と一緒に食事をすると言う物もあるが、決められた神饌であることが多く、'''直会'''とは異なる神事である。
また、神饌には季節の野菜、魚介類なども供えられるのでそれを調理した物が出される場合が多い。この為、神社によっては'''直会'''での料理は郷土料理と同一の場合がある。
神社から餅などを頂く場合、'''直らう'''と言う場合がある。これも似た意味である。
{{shinto-stub}} [[category:神道|なおらい]]

'''直会'''（なおらい）とは[[神社]]に於ける[[神事]]の内、神事が終了した後に神事に参加したもの一同で[[神饌]]を食する行事である。
神饌が食べられるものであるという証でもある。
墓参りの際に墓前にお供えしたものを、あとで食するというのと同じ行為であり、神霊が召し上がったものを頂くことにより、神霊との結びつきを強くし、神霊の力を分けてもらい、その加護を期待するのである。
神事によっては、神と一緒に食事をすると言う物もあるが、決められた神饌であることが多く、'''直会'''とは異なる神事である。
また、神饌には季節の野菜、魚介類なども供えられるのでそれを調理した物が出される場合が多い。この為、神社によっては'''直会'''での料理は郷土料理と同一の場合がある。
神社から餅などを頂く場合、'''直らう'''と言う場合がある。これも似た意味である。
{{shinto-stub}} [[category:神道|なおらい]] [[category:民俗学|なおらい]]

revision.comment: [[直会/削除]] を [[直会]] へ移動: 特定版削除対処終了
revision.text:
'''直会'''（なおらい）とは[[神社]]に於ける[[神事]]の内、神事が終了した後に神事に参加したもの一同で[[神饌]]を食する行事である。
神饌が食べられるものであるという証でもある。
墓参りの際に墓前にお供えしたものを、あとで食するというのと同じ行為であり、神霊が召し上がったものを頂くことにより、神霊との結びつきを強くし、神霊の力を分けてもらい、その加護を期待するのである。
神事によっては、神と一緒に食事をすると言う物もあるが、決められた神饌であることが多く、'''直会'''とは異なる神事である。
また、神饌には季節の野菜、魚介類なども供えられるのでそれを調理した物が出される場合が多い。この為、神社によっては'''直会'''での料理は郷土料理と同一の場合がある。
神社から餅などを頂く場合、'''直らう'''と言う場合がある。これも似た意味である。
{{shinto-stub}} [[category:神道|なおらい]] [[category:民俗学|なおらい]]

'''直会'''（なおらい）とは、[[神社]]に於ける[[祭|神事]]の最後に、神事に参加したもの一同で[[神酒]]を戴き[[神饌]]を食する行事である。一般には、神事が終わった後の宴会（打ち上げ）と解されているが、本来は神事を構成する行事の一つである。
墓参りの際に墓前にお供えしたものを、あとで食するというのと同じ行為であり、神霊が召し上がったものを頂くことにより、神霊との結びつきを強くし、神霊の力を分けてもらい、その加護を期待するのである。また、神饌が食べられるものであるという証でもある。神社から餅などを頂く場合、'''直らう'''と言う場合がある。これも似た意味である。
[[神社本庁]]が定める「神社祭式」では、どの祭式・祭祀においても必ず直会を行うこととしており、その具体的な作法も定めている。祭祀によっては故実による独自の作法が伝わっているものもある。
神饌には季節の野菜、魚介類なども供えられるのでそれを調理した物が出される場合が多い。この為、神社によっては'''直会'''での料理は郷土料理と同一の場合がある。
神事によっては祭祀者が神と一緒に食事をすると言う物もあるが、決められた神饌であることが多く、直会とは異なる神事である。
「なおらい（なほらい）」の語源については、「直り合い（なほりあひ）」の意とするのが通例である。一同が、祭祀のため行った斎戒を解き、平常に直る（復る）という意味である。[[折口信夫]]は、「なおらい」は[[直毘神|直日神]]を祀る神事であり、神祭が終わった後に、直日神を祀って神祭での誤ちを正すのだという説を唱えている。また、「なほる」を「食膳に着座する」、「あふ」を「一同が会する」の意とし、単に「神事が終わった後で神酒や神饌を飲食する」という意味で、解戒の意味はないとする説もある。
{{shinto-stub}} [[category:神道|なおらい]] [[category:民俗学|なおらい]]

'''直会'''（なおらい）とは、[[神社]]に於ける[[祭|神事]]の最後に、神事に参加したもの一同で[[神酒]]を戴き[[神饌]]を食する行事である。一般には、神事が終わった後の宴会（打ち上げ）と解されているが、本来は神事を構成する行事の一つである。

[[神社本庁]]が定める「神社祭式」では、どの祭式・祭祀においても必ず直会を行うこととしており、その具体的な作法も定めている。祭祀によっては故実による独自の作法が伝わっているものもある。

{{神道}} '''直会'''（なおらい）とは、[[神社]]に於ける[[祭|神事]]の最後に、神事に参加したもの一同で[[神酒]]を戴き[[神饌]]を食する行事（共飲共食儀礼）である。
一般には、神事が終わった後の宴会（打ち上げ）と解されているが、本来は神事を構成する行事の一つである。
<!-- 墓参りの際に墓前に[[供物|お供えしたもの]]を、あとで食するというのと同じ行為であり、（神道のみの習慣では？仏教では仏壇の下げ物とそれとは意味が違うはずですが？）-->神霊が召し上がったものを頂くことにより、神霊との結びつきを強くし、神霊の力を分けてもらい、その加護を期待するのである。また、神饌が食べられるものであるという証でもある。神社から餅などを頂く場合、'''直らう'''と言う場合がある。これも似た意味である。
[[神社本庁]]が定める「神社祭式」では、どの祭式・祭祀においても必ず直会を行うこととしており、その具体的な作法も定めている。祭祀によっては故実による独自の作法が伝わっているものもある。
神饌には季節の野菜、魚介類なども供えられるのでそれを調理した物が出される場合が多い。この為、神社によっては'''直会'''での料理は郷土料理と同一の場合がある。
神事によっては祭祀者が神と一緒に食事をすると言う物もあるが、決められた神饌であることが多く、直会とは異なる神事である。
「なほらひ（なおらい）」の語源については、「直り合い（なほりあひ）」の意とするのが通例である。
一同が、祭祀のため行った斎戒を解き、平常に直る（復る）という意味である。[[折口信夫]]は「なほらひ」は[[直毘神|直日神]]を祀る神事であり、神祭が終わった後に、直日神を祀って神祭での過ちを正すのだという説を唱えている。
また、「なほる」を「食膳に着座する」、「あふ」を「一同が会する」の意とし、単に「神事が終わった後で神酒や神饌を飲食する」という意味で、解戒の意味はないとする説もある。

神饌には季節の野菜、魚介類なども供えられるのでそれを調理した物が出される場合が多い。この為、神社によっては'''直会'''での料理は郷土料理と同一の場合がある。
神事によっては祭祀者が神と一緒に食事をすると言う物もあるが、決められた神饌であることが多く、直会とは異なる神事である。
== 語源 == 「なほらひ（なおらい）」の語源については、「直り合い（なほりあひ）」の意とするのが通例である。{{誰2|date=2010年12月}}
一同が、祭祀のため行った斎戒を解き、平常に直る（復る）という意味である。[[折口信夫]]は「なほらひ」は[[直毘神|直日神]]を祀る神事であり、神祭が終わった後に、直日神を祀って神祭での過ちを正すのだという説を唱えている。
また、「なほる」を「食膳に着座する」、「あふ」を「一同が会する」の意とし、単に「神事が終わった後で神酒や神饌を飲食する」という意味で、解戒の意味はないとする説もある。
{{shinto-stub}} [[category:神道|なおらい]] [[category:民俗学|なおらい]] {{DEFAULTSORT:なおらい}}

{{神道}} '''直会'''（なおらい）とは、[[神社]]に於ける[[祭|神事]]の最後に、神事に参加したもの一同で[[神酒]]を戴き[[神饌]]を食する行事（共飲共食儀礼）である。
== 概要 == 一般には、神事が終わった後の宴会（打ち上げ）と解されているが、本来は神事を構成する行事の一つである。神霊が召し上がったものを頂くことにより、神霊との結びつきを強くし、神霊の力を分けてもらい、その加護を期待するのである。また、神饌が食べられるものであるという証でもある。神社から餅などを頂く場合、'''直らう'''と言う場合がある。これも似た意味である。<!-- 墓参りの際に墓前に[[供物|お供えしたもの]]を、あとで食するというのと同じ行為であり、（神道のみの習慣では？仏教では仏壇の下げ物とそれとは意味が違うはずですが？）独自研究⇒削除-->
[[神社本庁]]が定める「神社祭式」では、どの祭式・祭祀においても必ず直会を行うこととしており、その具体的な作法も定めている。祭祀によっては故実による独自の作法が伝わっているものもある。
神饌には季節の野菜・魚介類なども供えられるのでそれを調理した物が出される場合が多い。このため、神社によっては'''直会'''での料理は郷土料理と同一の場合がある。
神事によっては祭祀者が神と一緒に食事をすると言う物もあるが、決められた神饌であることが多く、直会とは異なる神事である。
== 語源 == 「なほらひ（なおらい）」の語源については、「直り合い（なほりあひ）」の意とするのが通例である。{{誰2|date=2010年12月}}
一同が、祭祀のため行った斎戒を解き、平常に直る（復る）という意味である。[[折口信夫]]は「なほらひ」は[[直毘神|直日神]]を祀る神事であり、神祭が終わった後に、直日神を祀って神祭での過ちを正すのだという説を唱えている。
また、「なほる」を「食膳に着座する」、「あふ」を「一同が会する」の意とし、単に「神事が終わった後で神酒や神饌を飲食する」という意味で、解戒の意味はないとする説もある。

[[ファイル:Votive offering of the Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|[[神饌]]（[[香取神宮]]）]] {{神道}} '''直会'''（なおらい）とは、[[神社]]に於ける[[祭|神事]]の最後に、神事に参加したもの一同で[[神酒]]を戴き[[神饌]]を食する行事（共飲共食儀礼）である。

神社から餅などを頂く場合にいう'''直らう'''も似た意味の言葉である。
[[神社本庁]]が定める「神社祭式」では、どの祭式・祭祀でも必ず直会を行うとし、その具体的な作法も定めている。祭祀によっては故実による独自の作法が伝わっているものもある。
季節の野菜・魚介類などが神饌として供えられた場合は、それらを調理した物が出される場合が多い。このため、神社によっては'''直会'''での料理は郷土料理と同一の場合がある。
神事によっては祭祀者が神と一緒に食事をすると言う物もあるが、この場合は決められた神饌であることが多く、直会とは異なる神事である。

一同が、祭祀のため行った斎戒を解き、平常に直る（復る）という意味である。[[折口信夫]]は「なほらひ」は[[直毘神|直日神]]を祀る神事であり、神祭が終わった後に、直日神を祀って神祭での過ちを正すのだという説を唱えている。

[[ファイル:Votive offering of the Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|[[神饌]]（[[香取神宮]]）]] {{神道}} '''直会'''（なおらい）とは、[[神社]]に於ける[[祭|神事]]の最後に、神事に参加したもの一同で[[神酒]]を戴き[[神饌]]を食する行事（共飲共食儀礼）である。
== 概要 == 一般には、神事終了後の宴会（打ち上げ）とされるが、本来は神事を構成する行事の一つである。神霊が召し上がったものを頂くことにより、神霊との結びつきを強くし、神霊の力を分けてもらい、その加護を期待する。[[本居宣長]]は斎戒を解くこと（解斎）の意としたが、古くからの祭式では祭典の前儀や本儀で行われる例もあり、解斎のみを意味すると解するのは誤りである<ref>{{cite|和書|author=[[沼部春友]]|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=260}}</ref>
神社から餅などを頂く場合にいう'''直らう'''も似た意味の言葉である。
[[神社本庁]]が定める「神社祭式」では、どの祭式・祭祀でも必ず直会を行うとし、その具体的な作法も定めている。なお祭祀によっては故実による独自の作法が伝わっているものもあり、この場合はそれに従うものとされている。
季節の野菜・魚介類などが神饌として供えられた場合は、それらを調理した物が出される場合が多い。このため、神社によっては'''直会'''での料理は郷土料理と同一の場合がある。
神事によっては祭祀者が神と一緒に食事をすると言う物もあるが、この場合は決められた神饌であることが多く、直会とは異なる神事である。
== 語源 == 「なほらひ（なおらい）」の語源については、「直り合い（なほりあひ）」の意とするのが通例である。<ref group="注釈">[[本居宣長]]は「奈保理阿比（なほりあひ）の切（つづま）れる也（なり）、 直（なほ）るとは、斎（ものいみ）をゆるべて、 平常（つね）に復（かへ）る意也」と指摘している。 （『[[続日本紀|続紀]]歴朝詔詞解（しょっきれきちょうしょうしかい）』）</ref>一同が、祭祀のため行った斎戒を解き、平常に直る（復る）という意味である。
一方、『[[日本書紀]]』に「嘗」をナメライ、『[[江家次第]]』に「ナフアイの御粥」などとあることから、神人共食の「嘗め合い」が原義であるとする説もある<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=直会|editor=桜井徳太郎|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|pages=211, 212}}</ref>。
[[折口信夫]]は「なほらひ」は[[直毘神|直日神]]を祀る神事であり、神祭が終わった後に、直日神を祀って神祭での過ちを正すのだという説を唱えている。
また、「なほる」を「食膳に着座する」、「あふ」を「一同が会する」の意とし、単に「神事が終わった後で神酒や神饌を飲食する」という意味で、解戒の意味はないとする説もある。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} ===注釈=== <references group="注釈" /> ===参考文献=== <references />

神事によっては祭祀者が神と一緒に食事をすると言う物もあるが、この場合は決められた神饌であることが多く、直会とは異なる神事である。
== 語源 == 「なほらひ（なおらい）」の語源については、「直り合い（なほりあひ）」の意とするのが通例である。<ref group="注釈">[[本居宣長]]は「奈保理阿比（なほりあひ）の切（つづま）れる也（なり）、 直（なほ）るとは、斎（ものいみ）をゆるべて、 平常（つね）に復（かへ）る意也」と指摘している。 （『[[続日本紀|続紀]]歴朝詔詞解（しょっきれきちょうしょうしかい）』）</ref>一同が、祭祀のため行った斎戒を解き、平常に直る（復る）という意味である。
一方、『[[日本書紀]]』に「嘗」をナメライ、『[[江家次第]]』に「ナフアイの御粥」などとあることから、神人共食の「嘗め合い」が原義であるとする説もある<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=直会|editor=桜井徳太郎|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|pages=211, 212}}</ref>。
[[折口信夫]]は「なほらひ」は[[直毘神|直日神]]を祀る神事であり、神祭が終わった後に、直日神を祀って神祭での過ちを正すのだという説を唱えている。
また、「なほる」を「食膳に着座する」、「あふ」を「一同が会する」の意とし、単に「神事が終わった後で神酒や神饌を飲食する」という意味で、解戒の意味はないとする説もある。
==次第== 上記「神社祭式」に定められた次第としては、配膳所役が各人の前に饗膳を据え、ついで銚子所役が神酒を注ぎ、各人がこれを飲み、箸をつけることとなっている。この後、楽を奏し、行事終了後、膳を徹することとなっているが、楽は省略してもよいとされている。
膳には[[折敷]]を用い、品目は洗米、切りスルメ、切り昆布などである。神酒を注ぐには[[長柄の銚子]]を用い、三度に分けて注ぐ。神酒を受けるものは、銚子所役が前に来て跪いたとき、拍手を一つ打った後、杯をとって神酒を受ける。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} ===注釈=== <references group="注釈" /> ===参考文献=== <references />
{{shinto-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:なおらい}} [[category:神道]] [[Category:神道行事]] [[Category:儀式]] [[category:民俗学]]

[[ファイル:Votive offering of the Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|[[神饌]]（[[香取神宮]]）]] {{神道}} '''直会'''（なおらい）とは、[[神社]]に於ける[[祭|神事]]の最後に、神事に参加したもの一同で[[神酒]]を戴き[[神饌]]を食する行事（[[共飲共食]]儀礼）である。
== 概要 == 一般には、神事終了後の宴会（打ち上げ）とされるが、本来は神事を構成する行事の一つである。神霊が召し上がったものを頂くことにより、神霊との結びつきを強くし、神霊の力を分けてもらい、その加護を期待する。[[本居宣長]]は斎戒を解くこと（解斎）の意としたが、古くからの祭式では祭典の前儀や本儀で行われる例もあり、解斎のみを意味すると解するのは誤りである<ref name="教本">{{cite|和書|author=[[沼部春友]]|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|pages=260--266}}</ref>
神社から餅などを頂く場合にいう'''直らう'''も似た意味の言葉である。
[[神社本庁]]が定める「神社祭式」では、どの祭式・祭祀でも必ず直会を行うとし、その具体的な作法も定めている。なお祭祀によっては故実による独自の作法が伝わっているものもあり、この場合はそれに従うものとされている。
季節の野菜・魚介類などが神饌として供えられた場合は、それらを調理した物が出される場合が多い。このため、神社によっては'''直会'''での料理は郷土料理と同一の場合がある。
神事によっては祭祀者が神と一緒に食事をすると言う物もあるが、この場合は決められた神饌であることが多く、直会とは異なる神事である。

一方、『[[日本書紀]]』に「嘗」をナメライ、『[[江家次第]]』に「ナフアイの御粥」などとあることから、神人共食の「嘗め合い」が原義であるとする説もある<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=直会|editor=桜井徳太郎|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|pages=211, 212}}</ref>。
[[折口信夫]]は「なほらひ」は[[直毘神|直日神]]を祀る神事であり、神祭が終わった後に、直日神を祀って神祭での過ちを正すのだという説を唱えている。
また、「なほる」を「食膳に着座する」、「あふ」を「一同が会する」の意とし、単に「神事が終わった後で神酒や神饌を飲食する」という意味で、解戒の意味はないとする説もある。
== 次第 == 上記「神社祭式」に定められた次第としては、配膳所役が各人の前に饗膳を据え、ついで銚子所役が神酒を注ぎ、各人がこれを飲み、箸をつけることとなっている。この後、楽を奏し、行事終了後、膳を徹することとなっているが、楽は省略してもよいとされている。
膳には[[折敷]]を用い、品目は洗米、切りスルメ、切り昆布などである。神酒を注ぐには[[長柄の銚子]]を用い、三度に分けて注ぐ。神酒を受けるものは、銚子所役が前に来て跪いたとき、拍手を一つ打った後、杯をとって神酒を受ける。

{{複数の問題|出典の明記=2017年8月28日 (月) 08:04 (UTC)|独自研究=2017年8月28日 (月) 08:04 (UTC)}} [[ファイル:Votive offering of the Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|[[神饌]]（[[香取神宮]]）]] {{神道}} '''直会'''（なおらい）とは、[[神社]]に於ける[[祭祀 (神道)|祭祀]]の最後に、神事に参加したもの一同で[[神酒]]を戴き[[神饌]]を食する行事（[[共飲共食]]儀礼）である。
== 概要 == 一般には、神事終了後の宴会（打ち上げ）とされるが、本来は神事を構成する行事の一つである。神霊が召し上がったものを頂くことにより、神霊との結びつきを強くし、神霊の力を分けてもらい、その加護を期待する。[[本居宣長]]は斎戒を解くこと（解斎）の意としたが、古くからの祭式では祭典の前儀や本儀で行われる例もあり、解斎のみを意味すると解するのは誤りである<ref name="教本">{{cite|和書|author=[[沼部春友]]|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|pages=260--266}}</ref>
神社から餅などを頂く場合にいう'''直らう'''も似た意味の言葉である。
[[神社本庁]]が定める「神社祭式」では、どの祭式・祭祀でも必ず直会を行うとし、その具体的な作法も定めている。なお祭祀によっては故実による独自の作法が伝わっているものもあり、この場合はそれに従うものとされている。
季節の野菜・魚介類などが神饌として供えられた場合は、それらを調理した物が出される場合が多い。このため、神社によっては'''直会'''での料理は郷土料理と同一の場合がある。
神事によっては祭祀者が神と一緒に食事をすると言う物もあるが、この場合は決められた神饌であることが多く、直会とは異なる神事である。
== 語源 == 「なほらひ（なおらい）」の語源については、「直り合い（なほりあひ）」の意とするのが通例である。<ref group="注釈">[[本居宣長]]は「奈保理阿比（なほりあひ）の切（つづま）れる也（なり）、 直（なほ）るとは、斎（ものいみ）をゆるべて、 平常（つね）に復（かへ）る意也」と指摘している。 （『[[続日本紀|続紀]]歴朝詔詞解（しょっきれきちょうしょうしかい）』）</ref>一同が、祭祀のため行った斎戒を解き、平常に直る（復る）という意味である。
一方、『[[日本書紀]]』に「嘗」をナメライ、『[[江家次第]]』に「ナフアイの御粥」などとあることから、神人共食の「嘗め合い」が原義であるとする説もある<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=直会|editor=桜井徳太郎|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|pages=211, 212}}</ref>。
[[折口信夫]]は「なほらひ」は[[直毘神|直日神]]を祀る神事であり、神祭が終わった後に、直日神を祀って神祭での過ちを正すのだという説を唱えている。
また、「なほる」を「食膳に着座する」、「あふ」を「一同が会する」の意とし、単に「神事が終わった後で神酒や神饌を飲食する」という意味で、解戒の意味はないとする説もある。
== 次第 == 上記「神社祭式」に定められた次第としては、配膳所役が各人の前に饗膳を据え、ついで銚子所役が神酒を注ぎ、各人がこれを飲み、箸をつけることとなっている。この後、楽を奏し、行事終了後、膳を徹することとなっているが、楽は省略してもよいとされている。
膳には[[折敷]]を用い、品目は洗米、切りスルメ、切り昆布などである。神酒を注ぐには[[長柄の銚子]]を用い、三度に分けて注ぐ。神酒を受けるものは、銚子所役が前に来て跪いたとき、拍手を一つ打った後、杯をとって神酒を受ける。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === <references group="注釈" /> === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[鏡餅]] - [[鏡開き]] * [[聖体]] - [[聖餐]]
{{shinto-stub}}
{{デフォルトソート:なおらい}} [[category:神道]] [[Category:神道行事]] [[Category:儀式]] [[category:民俗学]]

季節の野菜・魚介類などが神饌として供えられた場合は、それらを調理した物が出される場合が多い。このため、神社によっては'''直会'''での料理は郷土料理と同一の場合がある。
神事によっては祭祀者が神と一緒に食事をすると言う物もあるが（[[顕斎]]）、この場合は決められた神饌であることが多く、直会とは異なる神事である。
== 語源 == 「なほらひ（なおらい）」の語源については、「直り合い（なほりあひ）」の意とするのが通例である。<ref group="注釈">[[本居宣長]]は「奈保理阿比（なほりあひ）の切（つづま）れる也（なり）、 直（なほ）るとは、斎（ものいみ）をゆるべて、 平常（つね）に復（かへ）る意也」と指摘している。 （『[[続日本紀|続紀]]歴朝詔詞解（しょっきれきちょうしょうしかい）』）</ref>一同が、祭祀のため行った斎戒を解き、平常に直る（復る）という意味である。
一方、『[[日本書紀]]』に「嘗」をナメライ、『[[江家次第]]』に「ナフアイの御粥」などとあることから、神人共食の「嘗め合い」が原義であるとする説もある<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=直会|editor=桜井徳太郎|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|pages=211, 212}}</ref>。
[[折口信夫]]は「なほらひ」は[[直毘神|直日神]]を祀る神事であり、神祭が終わった後に、直日神を祀って神祭での過ちを正すのだという説を唱えている。
また、「なほる」を「食膳に着座する」、「あふ」を「一同が会する」の意とし、単に「神事が終わった後で神酒や神饌を飲食する」という意味で、解戒の意味はないとする説もある。
== 次第 == 上記「神社祭式」に定められた次第としては、配膳所役が各人の前に饗膳を据え、ついで銚子所役が神酒を注ぎ、各人がこれを飲み、箸をつけることとなっている。この後、楽を奏し、行事終了後、膳を徹することとなっているが、楽は省略してもよいとされている。
膳には[[折敷]]を用い、品目は洗米、切りスルメ、切り昆布などである。神酒を注ぐには[[長柄の銚子]]を用い、三度に分けて注ぐ。神酒を受けるものは、銚子所役が前に来て跪いたとき、拍手を一つ打った後、杯をとって神酒を受ける。

revision.text:
{{複数の問題|出典の明記=2017年8月28日 (月) 08:04 (UTC)|独自研究=2017年8月28日 (月) 08:04 (UTC)}} [[ファイル:Votive offering of the Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|[[神饌]]（[[香取神宮]]）]] '''直会'''（なおらい）とは、[[神社]]に於ける[[祭祀 (神道)|祭祀]]の最後に、神事に参加したもの一同で[[神酒]]を戴き[[神饌]]を食する行事（[[共飲共食]]儀礼）である。
== 概要 == 一般には、神事終了後の宴会（打ち上げ）とされるが、本来は神事を構成する行事の一つである。神霊が召し上がったものを頂くことにより、神霊との結びつきを強くし、神霊の力を分けてもらい、その加護を期待する。[[本居宣長]]は斎戒を解くこと（解斎）の意としたが、古くからの祭式では祭典の前儀や本儀で行われる例もあり、解斎のみを意味すると解するのは誤りである<ref name="教本">{{cite|和書|author=[[沼部春友]]|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|pages=260--266}}</ref>
神社から餅などを頂く場合にいう'''直らう'''も似た意味の言葉である。
[[神社本庁]]が定める「神社祭式」では、どの祭式・祭祀でも必ず直会を行うとし、その具体的な作法も定めている。なお祭祀によっては故実による独自の作法が伝わっているものもあり、この場合はそれに従うものとされている。
季節の野菜・魚介類などが神饌として供えられた場合は、それらを調理した物が出される場合が多い。このため、神社によっては'''直会'''での料理は郷土料理と同一の場合がある。
神事によっては祭祀者が神と一緒に食事をすると言う物もあるが（[[顕斎]]）、この場合は決められた神饌であることが多く、直会とは異なる神事である。
== 語源 == 「なほらひ（なおらい）」の語源については、「直り合い（なほりあひ）」の意とするのが通例である。<ref group="注釈">[[本居宣長]]は「奈保理阿比（なほりあひ）の切（つづま）れる也（なり）、 直（なほ）るとは、斎（ものいみ）をゆるべて、 平常（つね）に復（かへ）る意也」と指摘している。 （『[[続日本紀|続紀]]歴朝詔詞解（しょっきれきちょうしょうしかい）』）</ref>一同が、祭祀のため行った斎戒を解き、平常に直る（復る）という意味である。
一方、『[[日本書紀]]』に「嘗」をナメライ、『[[江家次第]]』に「ナフアイの御粥」などとあることから、神人共食の「嘗め合い」が原義であるとする説もある<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=直会|editor=桜井徳太郎|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|pages=211, 212}}</ref>。
[[折口信夫]]は「なほらひ」は[[直毘神|直日神]]を祀る神事であり、神祭が終わった後に、直日神を祀って神祭での過ちを正すのだという説を唱えている。
また、「なほる」を「食膳に着座する」、「あふ」を「一同が会する」の意とし、単に「神事が終わった後で神酒や神饌を飲食する」という意味で、解戒の意味はないとする説もある。
== 次第 == 上記「神社祭式」に定められた次第としては、配膳所役が各人の前に饗膳を据え、ついで銚子所役が神酒を注ぎ、各人がこれを飲み、箸をつけることとなっている。この後、楽を奏し、行事終了後、膳を徹することとなっているが、楽は省略してもよいとされている。
膳には[[折敷]]を用い、品目は洗米、切りスルメ、切り昆布などである。神酒を注ぐには[[長柄の銚子]]を用い、三度に分けて注ぐ。神酒を受けるものは、銚子所役が前に来て跪いたとき、拍手を一つ打った後、杯をとって神酒を受ける。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === <references group="注釈" /> === 出典 === {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[鏡餅]] - [[鏡開き]] * [[聖体]] - [[聖餐]] {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:なおらい}} [[category:神道]] [[Category:神道行事]] [[Category:儀式]] [[category:民俗学]]

[[折口信夫]]は「なほらひ」は[[直毘神|直日神]]を祀る神事であり、神祭が終わった後に、直日神を祀って神祭での過ちを正すのだという説を唱えている。
また、「なほる」を「食膳に着座する」、「あふ」を「一同が会する」の意とし、単に「神事が終わった後で神酒や神饌を飲食する」という意味で、解戒の意味はないとする説もある。
== 次第 == 上記「神社祭式」に定められた次第としては、配膳所役が各人の前に饗膳を据え、ついで銚子所役が神酒を注ぎ、各人がこれを飲み、箸をつけることとなっている。この後、楽を奏し、行事終了後、膳を徹することとなっているが、楽は省略してもよいとされている。
膳には[[折敷]]を用い、品目は洗米、切りスルメ、切り昆布などである。神酒を注ぐには[[長柄の銚子]]を用い、三度に分けて注ぐ。神酒を受けるものは、銚子所役が前に来てひざまずいたとき、拍手を一つ打った後、杯をとって神酒を受ける。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === <references group="注釈" /> === 出典 === {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[鏡餅]] - [[鏡開き]] * [[聖体]] - [[聖餐]] {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:なおらい}} [[category:神道]] [[Category:神道行事]] [[Category:儀式]] [[category:民俗学]]

{{複数の問題|出典の明記=2017年8月28日 (月) 08:04 (UTC)|独自研究=2017年8月28日 (月) 08:04 (UTC)}} [[ファイル:Votive offering of the Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|[[神饌]]（[[香取神宮]]）|299x299ピクセル]] '''直会'''（なおらい）とは、[[神社]]における[[祭祀 (神道)|祭祀]]の最後に、神事に参加したもの一同で[[神酒]]をいただき[[神饌]]を食する行事（[[共飲共食]]儀礼）である。
== 概要 == 一般には、神事終了後の宴会（打ち上げ）とされるが、本来は神事を構成する行事の一つである。神霊が召し上がったものをいただくことにより、神霊との結びつきを強くし、神霊の力を分けてもらい、その加護を期待する。[[本居宣長]]は斎戒を解くこと（解斎）の意としたが、古くからの祭式では祭典の前儀や本儀で行われる例もあり、解斎のみを意味すると解するのは誤りである<ref name="教本">{{cite|和書|author=[[沼部春友]]|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|pages=260--266}}</ref>
神社から餅などをいただく場合にいう'''直らう'''も似た意味の言葉である。
[[神社本庁]]が定める「神社祭式」では、どの祭式・祭祀でも必ず直会を行うとし、その具体的な作法も定めている。なお祭祀によっては故実による独自の作法が伝わっているものもあり、この場合はそれに従うものとされている。
季節の野菜・魚介類などが神饌として供えられた場合は、それらを調理したものが出される場合が多い。このため、神社によっては'''直会'''での料理は郷土料理と同一の場合がある。
神事によっては祭祀者が神と一緒に食事をするというものもあるが（[[顕斎]]）、この場合は決められた神饌であることが多く、直会とは異なる神事である。
== 語源 == 「なほらひ（なおらい）」の語源については、「直り合い（なほりあひ）」の意とするのが通例である。<ref group="注釈">[[本居宣長]]は「奈保理阿比（なほりあひ）の切（つづま）れる也（なり）、 直（なほ）るとは、斎（ものいみ）をゆるべて、 平常（つね）に復（かへ）る意也」と指摘している。 （『[[続日本紀|続紀]]歴朝詔詞解（しょっきれきちょうしょうしかい）』）</ref>一同が、祭祀のため行った斎戒を解き、平常に直る（復る）という意味である。
一方、『[[日本書紀]]』に「嘗」をナメライ、『[[江家次第]]』に「ナフアイの御粥」などとあることから、神人共食の「嘗め合い」が原義であるとする説もある<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=直会|editor=桜井徳太郎|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|pages=211, 212}}</ref>。
[[折口信夫]]は「なほらひ」は[[直毘神|直日神]]を祀る神事であり、神祭が終わった後に、直日神を祀って神祭での過ちを正すのだという説を唱えている。
また、「なほる」を「食膳に着座する」、「あふ」を「一同が会する」の意とし、単に「神事が終わった後で神酒や神饌を飲食する」という意味で、解戒の意味はないとする説もある。
== 次第 == 上記「神社祭式」に定められた次第としては、配膳所役が各人の前に饗膳を据え、ついで銚子所役が神酒を注ぎ、各人がこれを飲み、箸をつけることとなっている。この後、楽を奏し、行事終了後、膳を徹することとなっているが、楽は省略してもよいとされている。
膳には[[折敷]]を用い、品目は洗米、切りスルメ、切り昆布などである。神酒を注ぐには[[長柄の銚子]]を用い、三度に分けて注ぐ。神酒を受けるものは、銚子所役が前に来てひざまずいたとき、拍手を一つ打った後、杯をとって神酒を受ける。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === <references group="注釈" /> === 出典 === {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[鏡餅]] - [[鏡開き]] * [[聖体]] - [[聖餐]] {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:なおらい}} [[category:神道]] [[Category:神道行事]] [[Category:儀式]] [[category:民俗学]]

revision.text:
{{Multiple issues|sources=August 28, 2017|original research=August 28, 2017}}
[[File:Votive offering of the Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|[[Shinsen]] ({{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}})|299x299px]] '''Naorai''' (直会) is a ritual conducted at the conclusion of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} ceremonies, during which participants drink {{ill|Shinshu (sacred sake)|ja|神酒}} and consume {{ill|Shinsen|ja|神饌}} as a form of communal dining and sharing (共飲共食 ritual).
== Overview ==
Although commonly seen as a celebratory banquet or after-party following a ceremony, naorai is an integral part of the Shinto ritual itself. By consuming the food and drink that were first offered to the deity, participants strengthen their connection with the divine spirit, receive its blessings, and anticipate its protection. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} interpreted it as a means of releasing oneself from ritual abstinence (解斎), but historical examples of naorai being performed during preparatory and principal rites suggest that interpreting it solely as 解斎 is a misunderstanding.<ref name="manual">{{cite|book|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2={{ill|Mogi Sadazumi|ja|茂木貞純}}|title=Shin Shinto Rituals and Ceremonial Guidebook|publisher=Eibun Kōshō Publishing|year=2011|pages=260–266}}</ref>
The term '''naorau''' (直らう), referring to receiving offerings such as rice cakes from a shrine, carries a similar meaning.
According to the guidelines of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}, naorai must be conducted in all Shinto rituals, with specific methods prescribed for its performance. However, some shrines follow traditional customs passed down through generations, and in such cases, those customs take precedence.
When seasonal vegetables, seafood, or other items are offered as shinsen, they are often cooked and served during naorai. Thus, at some shrines, the dishes served at naorai align with local cuisine.
Certain rituals involve priests dining with the deity ({{ill|Kenrei|ja|顕斎}}). While such rituals are distinct from naorai, they often use predetermined shinsen items.
== Etymology ==
The term "naorai" (なおらい) is generally understood to derive from "naori-ai" (直り合い), meaning "returning to normalcy together."<ref group="note">Motoori Norinaga explained, “Naorai is a contraction of ‘Naori-ai.’ The term ‘naori’ (straightening) signifies the act of releasing abstinence and returning to normalcy.” (From his commentary on the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}).</ref> It signifies the release of ritual abstinence and a return to daily life.
Alternatively, some scholars argue that its origin lies in the communal "naming and sharing" of food with the deity, as evidenced by terms like "namerai" in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} and "Naufai porridge" in the {{ill|Eke-shidai|ja|江家次第}}.<ref>{{cite|book|author=Jun'ichirō Ishikawa|contribution=Naorai|editor=Sakurai Tokutarō|title=Dictionary of Folk Beliefs|publisher=Tokyo-do Publishing|year=1980|pages=211–212}}</ref>
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} proposed that naorai is a ritual dedicated to the {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, conducted to correct any errors made during the preceding ceremony.
Another theory interprets "naoru" as "sitting down for a meal" and "au" as "gathering together," emphasizing the act of communal consumption of {{ill|Shinshu|ja|神酒}} and shinsen after the ritual, without the implication of 解戒 (release from abstinence).
== Procedure ==
According to the guidelines established in the "Shinto Rituals" by the Association of Shinto Shrines, the naorai process involves the following steps: 1. The ceremonial staff place the banquet trays before the participants. 2. The attendants pour sacred sake using a {{ill|Long-handled sake pourer|ja|長柄の銚子}}, dividing it into three pours. 3. Participants clap once, receive the sake, and begin eating the provided offerings.
The trays use {{ill|Oshiki|ja|折敷}}, with typical items including uncooked rice, cut squid, and cut kelp. Music may be performed during the ceremony but can be omitted.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kagami mochi|ja|鏡餅}} - {{ill|Kagami biraki|ja|鏡開き}} * {{ill|Eucharist|ja|聖体}} - {{ill|Holy Communion|ja|聖餐}}
{{Shinto horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}}
[[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto rituals]] [[Category:Ceremonies]] [[Category:Folklore]] [[ja:直会]]

{{Multiple issues|sources=August 28, 2017|original research=August 28, 2017}}
[[File:Votive offering of the Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|[[Shinsen]] ({{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}})|299x299px]] '''Naorai''' (直会) is a ritual conducted at the conclusion of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} ceremonies, during which participants drink {{ill|Shinshu (sacred sake)|ja|神酒}} and consume {{ill|Shinsen|ja|神饌}} as a form of communal dining and sharing (共飲共食 ritual).
== Overview ==
Although commonly seen as a celebratory banquet or after-party following a ceremony, naorai is an integral part of the Shinto ritual itself. By consuming the food and drink that were first offered to the deity, participants strengthen their connection with the divine spirit, receive its blessings, and anticipate its protection. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} interpreted it as a means of releasing oneself from ritual abstinence (解斎), but historical examples of naorai being performed during preparatory and principal rites suggest that interpreting it solely as 解斎 is a misunderstanding.<ref name="manual">{{cite|book|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2={{ill|Mogi Sadazumi|ja|茂木貞純}}|title=Shin Shinto Rituals and Ceremonial Guidebook|publisher=Eibun Kōshō Publishing|year=2011|pages=260–266}}</ref>
The term '''naorau''' (直らう), referring to receiving offerings such as rice cakes from a shrine, carries a similar meaning.
According to the guidelines of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}, naorai must be conducted in all Shinto rituals, with specific methods prescribed for its performance. However, some shrines follow traditional customs passed down through generations, and in such cases, those customs take precedence.
When seasonal vegetables, seafood, or other items are offered as shinsen, they are often cooked and served during naorai. Thus, at some shrines, the dishes served at naorai align with local cuisine.
Certain rituals involve priests dining with the deity ({{ill|Kenrei|ja|顕斎}}). While such rituals are distinct from naorai, they often use predetermined shinsen items.
== Etymology ==
The term "naorai" (なおらい) is generally understood to derive from "naori-ai" (直り合い), meaning "returning to normalcy together."<ref group="note">Motoori Norinaga explained, “Naorai is a contraction of ‘Naori-ai.’ The term ‘naori’ (straightening) signifies the act of releasing abstinence and returning to normalcy.” (From his commentary on the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}).</ref> It signifies the release of ritual abstinence and a return to daily life.
Alternatively, some scholars argue that its origin lies in the communal "naming and sharing" of food with the deity, as evidenced by terms like "namerai" in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} and "Naufai porridge" in the {{ill|Eke-shidai|ja|江家次第}}.<ref>{{cite|book|author=Jun'ichirō Ishikawa|contribution=Naorai|editor=Sakurai Tokutarō|title=Dictionary of Folk Beliefs|publisher=Tokyo-do Publishing|year=1980|pages=211–212}}</ref>
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} proposed that naorai is a ritual dedicated to the {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, conducted to correct any errors made during the preceding ceremony.
Another theory interprets "naoru" as "sitting down for a meal" and "au" as "gathering together," emphasizing the act of communal consumption of {{ill|Shinshu|ja|神酒}} and shinsen after the ritual, without the implication of 解戒 (release from abstinence).
== Procedure ==
According to the guidelines established in the "Shinto Rituals" by the Association of Shinto Shrines, the naorai process involves the following steps: 1. The ceremonial staff place the banquet trays before the participants. 2. The attendants pour sacred sake using a {{ill|Long-handled sake pourer|ja|長柄の銚子}}, dividing it into three pours. 3. Participants clap once, receive the sake, and begin eating the provided offerings.
The trays use {{ill|Oshiki|ja|折敷}}, with typical items including uncooked rice, cut squid, and cut kelp. Music may be performed during the ceremony but can be omitted.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kagami mochi|ja|鏡餅}} - {{ill|Kagami biraki|ja|鏡開き}} * {{ill|Eucharist|ja|聖体}} - {{ill|Holy Communion|ja|聖餐}}

revision.text:
{{Multiple issues|sources=August 28, 2017|original research=August 28, 2017}}
[[File:Votive offering of the Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|[[Shinsen]] ({{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}})|299x299px]] '''Naorai''' (直会) is a ritual conducted at the conclusion of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} ceremonies, during which participants drink {{ill|Shinshu (sacred sake)|ja|神酒}} and consume {{ill|Shinsen|ja|神饌}} as a form of communal dining and sharing (共飲共食 ritual).
== Overview ==
Although commonly seen as a celebratory banquet or after-party following a ceremony, naorai is an integral part of the Shinto ritual itself. By consuming the food and drink that were first offered to the deity, participants strengthen their connection with the divine spirit, receive its blessings, and anticipate its protection. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} interpreted it as a means of releasing oneself from ritual abstinence (解斎), but historical examples of naorai being performed during preparatory and principal rites suggest that interpreting it solely as 解斎 is a misunderstanding.<ref name="manual">{{cite|book|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2={{ill|Mogi Sadazumi|ja|茂木貞純}}|title=Shin Shinto Rituals and Ceremonial Guidebook|publisher=Eibun Kōshō Publishing|year=2011|pages=260–266}}</ref>
The term '''naorau''' (直らう), referring to receiving offerings such as rice cakes from a shrine, carries a similar meaning.
According to the guidelines of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}, naorai must be conducted in all Shinto rituals, with specific methods prescribed for its performance. However, some shrines follow traditional customs passed down through generations, and in such cases, those customs take precedence.
When seasonal vegetables, seafood, or other items are offered as shinsen, they are often cooked and served during naorai. Thus, at some shrines, the dishes served at naorai align with local cuisine.
Certain rituals involve priests dining with the deity ({{ill|Kenrei|ja|顕斎}}). While such rituals are distinct from naorai, they often use predetermined shinsen items.
== Etymology ==
The term "naorai" (なおらい) is generally understood to derive from "naori-ai" (直り合い), meaning "returning to normalcy together."<ref group="note">Motoori Norinaga explained, “Naorai is a contraction of ‘Naori-ai.’ The term ‘naori’ (straightening) signifies the act of releasing abstinence and returning to normalcy.” (From his commentary on the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}).</ref> It signifies the release of ritual abstinence and a return to daily life.
Alternatively, some scholars argue that its origin lies in the communal "naming and sharing" of food with the deity, as evidenced by terms like "namerai" in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} and "Naufai porridge" in the {{ill|Eke-shidai|ja|江家次第}}.<ref>{{cite|book|author=Jun'ichirō Ishikawa|contribution=Naorai|editor=Sakurai Tokutarō|title=Dictionary of Folk Beliefs|publisher=Tokyo-do Publishing|year=1980|pages=211–212}}</ref>
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} proposed that naorai is a ritual dedicated to the {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, conducted to correct any errors made during the preceding ceremony.
Another theory interprets "naoru" as "sitting down for a meal" and "au" as "gathering together," emphasizing the act of communal consumption of {{ill|Shinshu|ja|神酒}} and shinsen after the ritual, without the implication of 解戒 (release from abstinence).
== Procedure ==
According to the guidelines established in the "Shinto Rituals" by the Association of Shinto Shrines, the naorai process involves the following steps: 1. The ceremonial staff place the banquet trays before the participants. 2. The attendants pour sacred sake using a {{ill|Long-handled sake pourer|ja|長柄の銚子}}, dividing it into three pours. 3. Participants clap once, receive the sake, and begin eating the provided offerings.
The trays use {{ill|Oshiki|ja|折敷}}, with typical items including uncooked rice, cut squid, and cut kelp. Music may be performed during the ceremony but can be omitted.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kagami mochi|ja|鏡餅}} - {{ill|Kagami biraki|ja|鏡開き}} * {{ill|Eucharist|ja|聖体}} - {{ill|Holy Communion|ja|聖餐}}
{{Shinto horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}}

According to the guidelines established in the "Shinto Rituals" by the Association of Shinto Shrines, the naorai process involves the following steps: 1. Participants clap once, receive the sake, and begin eating the provided offerings.
The trays use {{ill|Oshiki|ja|折敷}}, with typical items including uncooked rice, cut squid, and cut kelp. Music may be performed during the ceremony but can be omitted.

The term "naorai" (なおらい) is generally understood to derive from "naori-ai" (直り合い), meaning "returning to normalcy together."<ref group="note">Motoori Norinaga explained, “Naorai is a contraction of ‘Naori-ai.’ The term ‘naori’ (straightening) signifies the act of releasing abstinence and returning to normalcy.” (From his commentary on the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}).</ref> It signifies the release of ritual abstinence and a return to daily life.
Alternatively, some scholars argue that its origin lies in the communal "naming and sharing" of food with the deity, as evidenced by terms like "namerai" in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} and "Naufai porridge" in the {{ill|Eke-shidai|ja|江家次第}}.<ref>{{cite|book|author=Jun'ichirō Ishikawa|contribution=Naorai|editor=Sakurai Tokutarō|title=Dictionary of Folk Beliefs|publisher=Tokyo-do Publishing|year=1980|pages=211–212}}</ref>
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} proposed that naorai is a ritual dedicated to the {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, conducted to correct any errors made during the preceding ceremony.
Another theory interprets "naoru" as "sitting down for a meal" and "au" as "gathering together," emphasizing the act of communal consumption of {{ill|Shinshu|ja|神酒}} and shinsen after the ritual, without the implication of 解戒 (release from abstinence).
== Procedure ==
According to the guidelines established in the "Shinto Rituals" by the Association of Shinto Shrines, the naorai process involves the following steps: 1. The ceremonial staff place the banquet trays before the participants. 2. The attendants pour sacred sake using a {{ill|Long-handled sake pourer|ja|長柄の銚子}}, dividing it into three pours. 3. Participants clap once, receive the sake, and begin eating the provided offerings.
The trays use {{ill|Oshiki|ja|折敷}}, with typical items including uncooked rice, cut squid, and cut kelp. Music may be performed during the ceremony but can be omitted.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kagami mochi|ja|鏡餅}} - {{ill|Kagami biraki|ja|鏡開き}} * {{ill|Eucharist|ja|聖体}} - {{ill|Holy Communion|ja|聖餐}}

{{Multiple issues|sources=August 28, 2017|original research=August 28, 2017}}
[[File:Votive offering of the Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|[[Shinsen]] ({{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}})|299x299px]] '''Naorai''' (直会) is a ritual conducted at the conclusion of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} ceremonies, during which participants drink {{ill|Shinshu (sacred sake)|ja|神酒}} and consume {{ill|Shinsen|ja|神饌}} as a form of communal dining and sharing (共飲共食 ritual).
== Overview ==
Although commonly seen as a celebratory banquet or after-party following a ceremony, naorai is an integral part of the Shinto ritual itself. By consuming the food and drink that were first offered to the deity, participants strengthen their connection with the divine spirit, receive its blessings, and anticipate its protection. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} interpreted it as a means of releasing oneself from ritual abstinence (解斎), but historical examples of naorai being performed during preparatory and principal rites suggest that interpreting it solely as 解斎 is a misunderstanding.<ref name="manual">{{cite|book|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2={{ill|Mogi Sadazumi|ja|茂木貞純}}|title=Shin Shinto Rituals and Ceremonial Guidebook|publisher=Eibun Kōshō Publishing|year=2011|pages=260–266}}</ref>
The term '''naorau''' (直らう), referring to receiving offerings such as rice cakes from a shrine, carries a similar meaning.
According to the guidelines of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}, naorai must be conducted in all Shinto rituals, with specific methods prescribed for its performance. However, some shrines follow traditional customs passed down through generations, and in such cases, those customs take precedence.
When seasonal vegetables, seafood, or other items are offered as shinsen, they are often cooked and served during naorai. Thus, at some shrines, the dishes served at naorai align with local cuisine.
Certain rituals involve priests dining with the deity ({{ill|Kenrei|ja|顕斎}}). While such rituals are distinct from naorai, they often use predetermined shinsen items.
== Etymology ==
The term "naorai" (なおらい) is generally understood to derive from "naori-ai" (直り合い), meaning "returning to normalcy together."<ref group="note">Motoori Norinaga explained, “Naorai is a contraction of ‘Naori-ai.’ The term ‘naori’ (straightening) signifies the act of releasing abstinence and returning to normalcy.” (From his commentary on the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}).</ref> It signifies the release of ritual abstinence and a return to daily life.
Alternatively, some scholars argue that its origin lies in the communal "naming and sharing" of food with the deity, as evidenced by terms like "namerai" in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} and "Naufai porridge" in the {{ill|Eke-shidai|ja|江家次第}}.<ref>{{cite|book|author=Jun'ichirō Ishikawa|contribution=Naorai|editor=Sakurai Tokutarō|title=Dictionary of Folk Beliefs|publisher=Tokyo-do Publishing|year=1980|pages=211–212}}</ref>
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} proposed that naorai is a ritual dedicated to the {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, conducted to correct any errors made during the preceding ceremony.
Another theory interprets "naoru" as "sitting down for a meal" and "au" as "gathering together," emphasizing the act of communal consumption of {{ill|Shinshu|ja|神酒}} and shinsen after the ritual, without the implication of 解戒 (release from abstinence).
== Procedure ==
According to the guidelines established in the "Shinto Rituals" by the Association of Shinto Shrines, the naorai process involves the following steps: 1. The ceremonial staff place the banquet trays before the participants. 2. The attendants pour sacred sake using a {{ill|Long-handled sake pourer|ja|長柄の銚子}}, dividing it into three pours. 3. Participants clap once, receive the sake, and begin eating the provided offerings.
The trays use {{ill|Oshiki|ja|折敷}}, with typical items including uncooked rice, cut squid, and cut kelp. Music may be performed during the ceremony but can be omitted.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kagami mochi|ja|鏡餅}} - {{ill|Kagami biraki|ja|鏡開き}} * {{ill|Eucharist|ja|聖体}} - {{ill|Holy Communion|ja|聖餐}}

The term "naorai" (なおらい) is generally understood to derive from "naori-ai" (直り合い), meaning "returning to normalcy together."<ref group="note">Motoori Norinaga explained, “Naorai is a contraction of ‘Naori-ai.’ The term ‘naori’ (straightening) signifies the act of releasing abstinence and returning to normalcy.” (From his commentary on the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}).</ref> It signifies the release of ritual abstinence and a return to daily life.
Alternatively, some scholars argue that its origin lies in the communal "naming and sharing" of food with the deity, as evidenced by terms like "namerai" in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} and "Naufai porridge" in the {{ill|Eke-shidai|ja|江家次第}}.<ref>{{cite|book|author=Jun'ichirō Ishikawa|contribution=Naorai|editor=Sakurai Tokutarō|title=Dictionary of Folk Beliefs|publisher=Tokyo-do Publishing|year=1980|pages=211–212}}</ref>
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} proposed that naorai is a ritual dedicated to the {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, conducted to correct any errors made during the preceding ceremony.
Another theory interprets "naoru" as "sitting down for a meal" and "au" as "gathering together," emphasizing the act of communal consumption of {{ill|Shinshu|ja|神酒}} and shinsen after the ritual, without the implication of 解戒 (release from abstinence).
== Procedure ==
According to the guidelines established in the "Shinto Rituals" by the Association of Shinto Shrines, the naorai process involves the following steps: 1. The ceremonial staff place the banquet trays before the participants. 2. The attendants pour sacred sake using a {{ill|Long-handled sake pourer|ja|長柄の銚子}}, dividing it into three pours. 3. Participants clap once, receive the sake, and begin eating the provided offerings.
The trays use {{ill|Oshiki|ja|折敷}}, with typical items including uncooked rice, cut squid, and cut kelp. Music may be performed during the ceremony but can be omitted.

[[File:Votive offering of the Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|[[Shinsen]] ({{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}})|299x299px]] '''Naorai''' (直会) is a ritual conducted at the conclusion of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} ceremonies, during which participants drink {{ill|Shinshu (sacred sake)|ja|神酒}} and consume {{ill|Shinsen|ja|神饌}} as a form of communal dining and sharing (共飲共食 ritual).
== Overview ==
Although commonly seen as a celebratory banquet or after-party following a ceremony, naorai is an integral part of the Shinto ritual itself. By consuming the food and drink that were first offered to the deity, participants strengthen their connection with the divine spirit, receive its blessings, and anticipate its protection. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} interpreted it as a means of releasing oneself from ritual abstinence (解斎), but historical examples of naorai being performed during preparatory and principal rites suggest that interpreting it solely as 解斎 is a misunderstanding.<ref name="manual">{{cite|book|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2={{ill|Mogi Sadazumi|ja|茂木貞純}}|title=Shin Shinto Rituals and Ceremonial Guidebook|publisher=Eibun Kōshō Publishing|year=2011|pages=260–266}}</ref>
The term '''naorau''' (直らう), referring to receiving offerings such as rice cakes from a shrine, carries a similar meaning.
According to the guidelines of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}, naorai must be conducted in all Shinto rituals, with specific methods prescribed for its performance. However, some shrines follow traditional customs passed down through generations, and in such cases, those customs take precedence.
When seasonal vegetables, seafood, or other items are offered as shinsen, they are often cooked and served during naorai. Thus, at some shrines, the dishes served at naorai align with local cuisine.
Certain rituals involve priests dining with the deity ({{ill|Kenrei|ja|顕斎}}). While such rituals are distinct from naorai, they often use predetermined shinsen items.
== Etymology ==
The term "naorai" (なおらい) is generally understood to derive from "naori-ai" (直り合い), meaning "returning to normalcy together."<ref group="note">Motoori Norinaga explained, “Naorai is a contraction of ‘Naori-ai.’ The term ‘naori’ (straightening) signifies the act of releasing abstinence and returning to normalcy.” (From his commentary on the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}).</ref> It signifies the release of ritual abstinence and a return to daily life.
Alternatively, some scholars argue that its origin lies in the communal "naming and sharing" of food with the deity, as evidenced by terms like "namerai" in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} and "Naufai porridge" in the {{ill|Eke-shidai|ja|江家次第}}.<ref>{{cite|book|author=Jun'ichirō Ishikawa|contribution=Naorai|editor=Sakurai Tokutarō|title=Dictionary of Folk Beliefs|publisher=Tokyo-do Publishing|year=1980|pages=211–212}}</ref>
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} proposed that naorai is a ritual dedicated to the {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, conducted to correct any errors made during the preceding ceremony.
Another theory interprets "naoru" as "sitting down for a meal" and "au" as "gathering together," emphasizing the act of communal consumption of {{ill|Shinshu|ja|神酒}} and shinsen after the ritual, without the implication of 解戒 (release from abstinence).
== Procedure ==
According to the guidelines established in the "Shinto Rituals" by the Association of Shinto Shrines, the naorai process involves the following steps: 1. The ceremonial staff place the banquet trays before the participants. 2. The attendants pour sacred sake using a {{ill|Long-handled sake pourer|ja|長柄の銚子}}, dividing it into three pours. 3. Participants clap once, receive the sake, and begin eating the provided offerings.
The trays use {{ill|Oshiki|ja|折敷}}, with typical items including uncooked rice, cut squid, and cut kelp. Music may be performed during the ceremony but can be omitted.
== Notes == <references group="note" />

[[ja:直会]]

{{Multiple issues|sources=August 28, 2017|original research=August 28, 2017}}
[[File:Votive offering of the Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|[[Shinsen]] ({{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}})|299x299px]] '''Naorai''' (直会) is a ritual conducted at the conclusion of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} ceremonies, during which participants drink {{ill|Shinshu (sacred sake)|ja|神酒}} and consume {{ill|Shinsen|ja|神饌}} as a form of communal dining and sharing (共飲共食 ritual).
== Overview ==
Although commonly seen as a celebratory banquet or after-party following a ceremony, naorai is an integral part of the Shinto ritual itself. By consuming the food and drink that were first offered to the deity, participants strengthen their connection with the divine spirit, receive its blessings, and anticipate its protection. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} interpreted it as a means of releasing oneself from ritual abstinence (解斎), but historical examples of naorai being performed during preparatory and principal rites suggest that interpreting it solely as 解斎 is a misunderstanding.<ref name="manual">{{cite|book|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2={{ill|Mogi Sadazumi|ja|茂木貞純}}|title=Shin Shinto Rituals and Ceremonial Guidebook|publisher=Eibun Kōshō Publishing|year=2011|pages=260–266}}</ref>
The term '''naorau''' (直らう), referring to receiving offerings such as rice cakes from a shrine, carries a similar meaning.
According to the guidelines of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}, naorai must be conducted in all Shinto rituals, with specific methods prescribed for its performance. However, some shrines follow traditional customs passed down through generations, and in such cases, those customs take precedence.
When seasonal vegetables, seafood, or other items are offered as shinsen, they are often cooked and served during naorai. Thus, at some shrines, the dishes served at naorai align with local cuisine.
Certain rituals involve priests dining with the deity ({{ill|Kenrei|ja|顕斎}}). While such rituals are distinct from naorai, they often use predetermined shinsen items.
== Etymology ==
The term "naorai" (なおらい) is generally understood to derive from "naori-ai" (直り合い), meaning "returning to normalcy together."<ref group="note">Motoori Norinaga explained, “Naorai is a contraction of ‘Naori-ai.’ The term ‘naori’ (straightening) signifies the act of releasing abstinence and returning to normalcy.” (From his commentary on the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}).</ref> It signifies the release of ritual abstinence and a return to daily life.
Alternatively, some scholars argue that its origin lies in the communal "naming and sharing" of food with the deity, as evidenced by terms like "namerai" in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} and "Naufai porridge" in the {{ill|Eke-shidai|ja|江家次第}}.<ref>{{cite|book|author=Jun'ichirō Ishikawa|contribution=Naorai|editor=Sakurai Tokutarō|title=Dictionary of Folk Beliefs|publisher=Tokyo-do Publishing|year=1980|pages=211–212}}</ref>
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} proposed that naorai is a ritual dedicated to the {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, conducted to correct any errors made during the preceding ceremony.
Another theory interprets "naoru" as "sitting down for a meal" and "au" as "gathering together," emphasizing the act of communal consumption of {{ill|Shinshu|ja|神酒}} and shinsen after the ritual, without the implication of 解戒 (release from abstinence).
== Procedure ==
According to the guidelines established in the "Shinto Rituals" by the Association of Shinto Shrines, the naorai process involves the following steps: 1. The ceremonial staff place the banquet trays before the participants. 2. The attendants pour sacred sake using a {{ill|Long-handled sake pourer|ja|長柄の銚子}}, dividing it into three pours. 3. Participants clap once, receive the sake, and begin eating the provided offerings.
The trays use {{ill|Oshiki|ja|折敷}}, with typical items including uncooked rice, cut squid, and cut kelp. Music may be performed during the ceremony but can be omitted.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kagami mochi|ja|鏡餅}} - {{ill|Kagami biraki|ja|鏡開き}} * {{ill|Eucharist|ja|聖体}} - {{ill|Holy Communion|ja|聖餐}}

== Overview ==
Although commonly seen as a celebratory banquet or after-party following a ceremony, naorai is an integral part of the Shinto ritual itself. By consuming the food and drink that were first offered to the deity, participants strengthen their connection with the divine spirit, receive its blessings, and anticipate its protection. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} interpreted it as a means of releasing oneself from ritual abstinence (解斎), but historical examples of naorai being performed during preparatory and principal rites suggest that interpreting it solely as 解斎 is a misunderstanding.<ref name="manual">{{cite|book|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2={{ill|Mogi Sadazumi|ja|茂木貞純}}|title=Shin Shinto Rituals and Ceremonial Guidebook|publisher=Eibun Kōshō Publishing|year=2011|pages=260–266}}</ref>
The term '''naorau''' (直らう), referring to receiving offerings such as rice cakes from a shrine, carries a similar meaning.
According to the guidelines of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}, naorai must be conducted in all Shinto rituals, with specific methods prescribed for its performance. However, some shrines follow traditional customs passed down through generations, and in such cases, those customs take precedence.
When seasonal vegetables, seafood, or other items are offered as shinsen, they are often cooked and served during naorai. Thus, at some shrines, the dishes served at naorai align with local cuisine.
Certain rituals involve priests dining with the deity ({{ill|Kenrei|ja|顕斎}}). While such rituals are distinct from naorai, they often use predetermined shinsen items.
== Etymology ==
The term "naorai" (なおらい) is generally understood to derive from "naori-ai" (直り合い), meaning "returning to normalcy together."<ref group="note">Motoori Norinaga explained, “Naorai is a contraction of ‘Naori-ai.’ The term ‘naori’ (straightening) signifies the act of releasing abstinence and returning to normalcy.” (From his commentary on the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}).</ref> It signifies the release of ritual abstinence and a return to daily life.
Alternatively, some scholars argue that its origin lies in the communal "naming and sharing" of food with the deity, as evidenced by terms like "namerai" in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} and "Naufai porridge" in the {{ill|Eke-shidai|ja|江家次第}}.<ref>{{cite|book|author=Jun'ichirō Ishikawa|contribution=Naorai|editor=Sakurai Tokutarō|title=Dictionary of Folk Beliefs|publisher=Tokyo-do Publishing|year=1980|pages=211–212}}</ref>
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} proposed that naorai is a ritual dedicated to the {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, conducted to correct any errors made during the preceding ceremony.
Another theory interprets "naoru" as "sitting down for a meal" and "au" as "gathering together," emphasizing the act of communal consumption of {{ill|Shinshu|ja|神酒}} and shinsen after the ritual, without the implication of 解戒 (release from abstinence).
== Procedure ==
According to the guidelines established in the "Shinto Rituals" by the Association of Shinto Shrines, the naorai process involves the following steps: 1. The ceremonial staff place the banquet trays before the participants. 2. The attendants pour sacred sake using a {{ill|Long-handled sake pourer|ja|長柄の銚子}}, dividing it into three pours. 3. Participants clap once, receive the sake, and begin eating the provided offerings.
The trays use {{ill|Oshiki|ja|折敷}}, with typical items including uncooked rice, cut squid, and cut kelp. Music may be performed during the ceremony but can be omitted.
== Notes == <references group="note" />

{{Multiple issues|sources=August 28, 2017|original research=August 28, 2017}}
[[File:Votive offering of the Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|[[Shinsen]] ({{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}})|299x299px]] '''Naorai''' (直会) is a ritual conducted at the conclusion of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} ceremonies, during which participants drink {{ill|Shinshu (sacred sake)|ja|神酒}} and consume {{ill|Shinsen|ja|神饌}} as a form of communal dining and sharing (共飲共食 ritual).
== Overview ==
Although commonly seen as a celebratory banquet or after-party following a ceremony, naorai is an integral part of the Shinto ritual itself. By consuming the food and drink that were first offered to the deity, participants strengthen their connection with the divine spirit, receive its blessings, and anticipate its protection. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} interpreted it as a means of releasing oneself from ritual abstinence (解斎), but historical examples of naorai being performed during preparatory and principal rites suggest that interpreting it solely as 解斎 is a misunderstanding.<ref name="manual">{{cite|book|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2={{ill|Mogi Sadazumi|ja|茂木貞純}}|title=Shin Shinto Rituals and Ceremonial Guidebook|publisher=Eibun Kōshō Publishing|year=2011|pages=260–266}}</ref>
The term '''naorau''' (直らう), referring to receiving offerings such as rice cakes from a shrine, carries a similar meaning.
According to the guidelines of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}, naorai must be conducted in all Shinto rituals, with specific methods prescribed for its performance. However, some shrines follow traditional customs passed down through generations, and in such cases, those customs take precedence.
When seasonal vegetables, seafood, or other items are offered as shinsen, they are often cooked and served during naorai. Thus, at some shrines, the dishes served at naorai align with local cuisine.
Certain rituals involve priests dining with the deity ({{ill|Kenrei|ja|顕斎}}). While such rituals are distinct from naorai, they often use predetermined shinsen items.
== Etymology ==
The term "naorai" (なおらい) is generally understood to derive from "naori-ai" (直り合い), meaning "returning to normalcy together."<ref group="note">Motoori Norinaga explained, “Naorai is a contraction of ‘Naori-ai.’ The term ‘naori’ (straightening) signifies the act of releasing abstinence and returning to normalcy.” (From his commentary on the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}).</ref> It signifies the release of ritual abstinence and a return to daily life.
Alternatively, some scholars argue that its origin lies in the communal "naming and sharing" of food with the deity, as evidenced by terms like "namerai" in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} and "Naufai porridge" in the {{ill|Eke-shidai|ja|江家次第}}.<ref>{{cite|book|author=Jun'ichirō Ishikawa|contribution=Naorai|editor=Sakurai Tokutarō|title=Dictionary of Folk Beliefs|publisher=Tokyo-do Publishing|year=1980|pages=211–212}}</ref>
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} proposed that naorai is a ritual dedicated to the {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, conducted to correct any errors made during the preceding ceremony.
Another theory interprets "naoru" as "sitting down for a meal" and "au" as "gathering together," emphasizing the act of communal consumption of {{ill|Shinshu|ja|神酒}} and shinsen after the ritual, without the implication of 解戒 (release from abstinence).
== Procedure ==
According to the guidelines established in the "Shinto Rituals" by the Association of Shinto Shrines, the naorai process involves the following steps: 1. The ceremonial staff place the banquet trays before the participants. 2. The attendants pour sacred sake using a {{ill|Long-handled sake pourer|ja|長柄の銚子}}, dividing it into three pours. 3. Participants clap once, receive the sake, and begin eating the provided offerings.
The trays use {{ill|Oshiki|ja|折敷}}, with typical items including uncooked rice, cut squid, and cut kelp. Music may be performed during the ceremony but can be omitted.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == {{Reflist}}

The term "naorai" (なおらい) is generally understood to derive from "naori-ai" (直り合い), meaning "returning to normalcy together."<ref group="note">Motoori Norinaga explained, “Naorai is a contraction of ‘Naori-ai.’ The term ‘naori’ (straightening) signifies the act of releasing abstinence and returning to normalcy.” (From his commentary on the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}).</ref> It signifies the release of ritual abstinence and a return to daily life.
Alternatively, some scholars argue that its origin lies in the communal "naming and sharing" of food with the deity, as evidenced by terms like "namerai" in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} and "Naufai porridge" in the {{ill|Eke-shidai|ja|江家次第}}.<ref>{{cite|book|author=Jun'ichirō Ishikawa|contribution=Naorai|editor=Sakurai Tokutarō|title=Dictionary of Folk Beliefs|publisher=Tokyo-do Publishing|year=1980|pages=211–212}}</ref>
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} proposed that naorai is a ritual dedicated to the {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, conducted to correct any errors made during the preceding ceremony.
Another theory interprets "naoru" as "sitting down for a meal" and "au" as "gathering together," emphasizing the act of communal consumption of {{ill|Shinshu|ja|神酒}} and shinsen after the ritual, without the implication of 解戒 (release from abstinence).
== Procedure ==
According to the guidelines established in the "Shinto Rituals" by the Association of Shinto Shrines, the naorai process involves the following steps: 1. The ceremonial staff place the banquet trays before the participants. 2. The attendants pour sacred sake using a {{ill|Long-handled sake pourer|ja|長柄の銚子}}, dividing it into three pours. 3. Participants clap once, receive the sake, and begin eating the provided offerings.
The trays use {{ill|Oshiki|ja|折敷}}, with typical items including uncooked rice, cut squid, and cut kelp. Music may be performed during the ceremony but can be omitted.

[[File:Votive offering of the Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|[[Shinsen]] ({{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}})|299x299px]] '''Naorai''' (直会) is a ritual conducted at the conclusion of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} ceremonies, during which participants drink {{ill|Shinshu (sacred sake)|ja|神酒}} and consume {{ill|Shinsen|ja|神饌}} as a form of communal dining and sharing (共飲共食 ritual).
== Overview ==
Although commonly seen as a celebratory banquet or after-party following a ceremony, naorai is an integral part of the Shinto ritual itself. By consuming the food and drink that were first offered to the deity, participants strengthen their connection with the divine spirit, receive its blessings, and anticipate its protection. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} interpreted it as a means of releasing oneself from ritual abstinence (解斎), but historical examples of naorai being performed during preparatory and principal rites suggest that interpreting it solely as 解斎 is a misunderstanding.<ref name="manual">{{cite|book|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2={{ill|Mogi Sadazumi|ja|茂木貞純}}|title=Shin Shinto Rituals and Ceremonial Guidebook|publisher=Eibun Kōshō Publishing|year=2011|pages=260–266}}</ref>
The term '''naorau''' (直らう), referring to receiving offerings such as rice cakes from a shrine, carries a similar meaning.
According to the guidelines of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}, naorai must be conducted in all Shinto rituals, with specific methods prescribed for its performance. However, some shrines follow traditional customs passed down through generations, and in such cases, those customs take precedence.
When seasonal vegetables, seafood, or other items are offered as shinsen, they are often cooked and served during naorai. Thus, at some shrines, the dishes served at naorai align with local cuisine.
Certain rituals involve priests dining with the deity ({{ill|Kenrei|ja|顕斎}}). While such rituals are distinct from naorai, they often use predetermined shinsen items.
== Etymology ==
The term "naorai" (なおらい) is generally understood to derive from "naori-ai" (直り合い), meaning "returning to normalcy together."<ref group="note">Motoori Norinaga explained, “Naorai is a contraction of ‘Naori-ai.’ The term ‘naori’ (straightening) signifies the act of releasing abstinence and returning to normalcy.” (From his commentary on the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}).</ref> It signifies the release of ritual abstinence and a return to daily life.
Alternatively, some scholars argue that its origin lies in the communal "naming and sharing" of food with the deity, as evidenced by terms like "namerai" in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} and "Naufai porridge" in the {{ill|Eke-shidai|ja|江家次第}}.<ref>{{cite|book|author=Jun'ichirō Ishikawa|contribution=Naorai|editor=Sakurai Tokutarō|title=Dictionary of Folk Beliefs|publisher=Tokyo-do Publishing|year=1980|pages=211–212}}</ref>
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} proposed that naorai is a ritual dedicated to the {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, conducted to correct any errors made during the preceding ceremony.
Another theory interprets "naoru" as "sitting down for a meal" and "au" as "gathering together," emphasizing the act of communal consumption of {{ill|Shinshu|ja|神酒}} and shinsen after the ritual, without the implication of 解戒 (release from abstinence).
== Procedure ==
According to the guidelines established in the "Shinto Rituals" by the Association of Shinto Shrines, the naorai process involves the following steps: 1. The ceremonial staff place the banquet trays before the participants. 2. The attendants pour sacred sake using a {{ill|Long-handled sake pourer|ja|長柄の銚子}}, dividing it into three pours. 3. Participants clap once, receive the sake, and begin eating the provided offerings.
The trays use {{ill|Oshiki|ja|折敷}}, with typical items including uncooked rice, cut squid, and cut kelp. Music may be performed during the ceremony but can be omitted.

{{Multiple issues|sources=August 28, 2017|original research=August 28, 2017}}
[[File:Votive offering of the Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|[[Shinsen]] ({{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}})|299x299px]] '''Naorai''' (直会) is a ritual conducted at the conclusion of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} ceremonies, during which participants drink {{ill|Shinshu (sacred sake)|ja|神酒}} and consume {{ill|Shinsen|ja|神饌}} as a form of communal dining and sharing (共飲共食 ritual).
== Overview ==
Although commonly seen as a celebratory banquet or after-party following a ceremony, naorai is an integral part of the Shinto ritual itself. By consuming the food and drink that were first offered to the deity, participants strengthen their connection with the divine spirit, receive its blessings, and anticipate its protection. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} interpreted it as a means of releasing oneself from ritual abstinence (解斎), but historical examples of naorai being performed during preparatory and principal rites suggest that interpreting it solely as 解斎 is a misunderstanding.<ref name="manual">{{cite|book|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2={{ill|Mogi Sadazumi|ja|茂木貞純}}|title=Shin Shinto Rituals and Ceremonial Guidebook|publisher=Eibun Kōshō Publishing|year=2011|pages=260–266}}</ref>
The term '''naorau''' (直らう), referring to receiving offerings such as rice cakes from a shrine, carries a similar meaning.
According to the guidelines of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}, naorai must be conducted in all Shinto rituals, with specific methods prescribed for its performance. However, some shrines follow traditional customs passed down through generations, and in such cases, those customs take precedence.
When seasonal vegetables, seafood, or other items are offered as shinsen, they are often cooked and served during naorai. Thus, at some shrines, the dishes served at naorai align with local cuisine.
Certain rituals involve priests dining with the deity ({{ill|Kenrei|ja|顕斎}}). While such rituals are distinct from naorai, they often use predetermined shinsen items.
== Etymology ==
The term "naorai" (なおらい) is generally understood to derive from "naori-ai" (直り合い), meaning "returning to normalcy together."<ref group="note">Motoori Norinaga explained, “Naorai is a contraction of ‘Naori-ai.’ The term ‘naori’ (straightening) signifies the act of releasing abstinence and returning to normalcy.” (From his commentary on the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}).</ref> It signifies the release of ritual abstinence and a return to daily life.
Alternatively, some scholars argue that its origin lies in the communal "naming and sharing" of food with the deity, as evidenced by terms like "namerai" in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} and "Naufai porridge" in the {{ill|Eke-shidai|ja|江家次第}}.<ref>{{cite|book|author=Jun'ichirō Ishikawa|contribution=Naorai|editor=Sakurai Tokutarō|title=Dictionary of Folk Beliefs|publisher=Tokyo-do Publishing|year=1980|pages=211–212}}</ref>
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} proposed that naorai is a ritual dedicated to the {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, conducted to correct any errors made during the preceding ceremony.
Another theory interprets "naoru" as "sitting down for a meal" and "au" as "gathering together," emphasizing the act of communal consumption of {{ill|Shinshu|ja|神酒}} and shinsen after the ritual, without the implication of 解戒 (release from abstinence).
== Procedure ==
According to the guidelines established in the "Shinto Rituals" by the Association of Shinto Shrines, the naorai process involves the following steps: 1. The ceremonial staff place the banquet trays before the participants. 2. The attendants pour sacred sake using a {{ill|Long-handled sake pourer|ja|長柄の銚子}}, dividing it into three pours. 3. Participants clap once, receive the sake, and begin eating the provided offerings.
The trays use {{ill|Oshiki|ja|折敷}}, with typical items including uncooked rice, cut squid, and cut kelp. Music may be performed during the ceremony but can be omitted.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kagami mochi|ja|鏡餅}} - {{ill|Kagami biraki|ja|鏡開き}} * {{ill|Eucharist|ja|聖体}} - {{ill|Holy Communion|ja|聖餐}}

{{Multiple issues|sources=August 28, 2017|original research=August 28, 2017}}
[[File:Votive offering of the Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|[[Shinsen]] ({{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}})|299x299px]] '''Naorai''' (直会) is a ritual conducted at the conclusion of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} ceremonies, during which participants drink {{ill|Shinshu (sacred sake)|ja|神酒}} and consume {{ill|Shinsen|ja|神饌}} as a form of communal dining and sharing (共飲共食 ritual).
== Overview ==
Although commonly seen as a celebratory banquet or after-party following a ceremony, naorai is an integral part of the Shinto ritual itself. By consuming the food and drink that were first offered to the deity, participants strengthen their connection with the divine spirit, receive its blessings, and anticipate its protection. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} interpreted it as a means of releasing oneself from ritual abstinence (解斎), but historical examples of naorai being performed during preparatory and principal rites suggest that interpreting it solely as 解斎 is a misunderstanding.<ref name="manual">{{cite|book|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2={{ill|Mogi Sadazumi|ja|茂木貞純}}|title=Shin Shinto Rituals and Ceremonial Guidebook|publisher=Eibun Kōshō Publishing|year=2011|pages=260–266}}</ref>
The term '''naorau''' (直らう), referring to receiving offerings such as rice cakes from a shrine, carries a similar meaning.
According to the guidelines of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}, naorai must be conducted in all Shinto rituals, with specific methods prescribed for its performance. However, some shrines follow traditional customs passed down through generations, and in such cases, those customs take precedence.
When seasonal vegetables, seafood, or other items are offered as shinsen, they are often cooked and served during naorai. Thus, at some shrines, the dishes served at naorai align with local cuisine.
Certain rituals involve priests dining with the deity ({{ill|Kenrei|ja|顕斎}}). While such rituals are distinct from naorai, they often use predetermined shinsen items.
== Etymology ==
The term "naorai" (なおらい) is generally understood to derive from "naori-ai" (直り合い), meaning "returning to normalcy together."<ref group="note">Motoori Norinaga explained, “Naorai is a contraction of ‘Naori-ai.’ The term ‘naori’ (straightening) signifies the act of releasing abstinence and returning to normalcy.” (From his commentary on the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}).</ref> It signifies the release of ritual abstinence and a return to daily life.
Alternatively, some scholars argue that its origin lies in the communal "naming and sharing" of food with the deity, as evidenced by terms like "namerai" in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} and "Naufai porridge" in the {{ill|Eke-shidai|ja|江家次第}}.<ref>{{cite|book|author=Jun'ichirō Ishikawa|contribution=Naorai|editor=Sakurai Tokutarō|title=Dictionary of Folk Beliefs|publisher=Tokyo-do Publishing|year=1980|pages=211–212}}</ref>
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} proposed that naorai is a ritual dedicated to the {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, conducted to correct any errors made during the preceding ceremony.
Another theory interprets "naoru" as "sitting down for a meal" and "au" as "gathering together," emphasizing the act of communal consumption of {{ill|Shinshu|ja|神酒}} and shinsen after the ritual, without the implication of 解戒 (release from abstinence).
== Procedure ==
According to the guidelines established in the "Shinto Rituals" by the Association of Shinto Shrines, the naorai process involves the following steps: 1. The ceremonial staff place the banquet trays before the participants. 2. The attendants pour sacred sake using a {{ill|Long-handled sake pourer|ja|長柄の銚子}}, dividing it into three pours. 3. Participants clap once, receive the sake, and begin eating the provided offerings.
The trays use {{ill|Oshiki|ja|折敷}}, with typical items including uncooked rice, cut squid, and cut kelp. Music may be performed during the ceremony but can be omitted.
== Notes == <references group="note" />

[[File:Votive offering of the Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|[[Shinsen]] ({{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}})|299x299px]] '''Naorai''' (直会) is a ritual conducted at the conclusion of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} ceremonies, during which participants drink {{ill|Shinshu (sacred sake)|ja|神酒}} and consume {{ill|Shinsen|ja|神饌}} as a form of communal dining and sharing (共飲共食 ritual).
== Overview ==
Although commonly seen as a celebratory banquet or after-party following a ceremony, naorai is an integral part of the Shinto ritual itself. By consuming the food and drink that were first offered to the deity, participants strengthen their connection with the divine spirit, receive its blessings, and anticipate its protection. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} interpreted it as a means of releasing oneself from ritual abstinence (解斎), but historical examples of naorai being performed during preparatory and principal rites suggest that interpreting it solely as 解斎 is a misunderstanding.<ref name="manual">{{cite|book|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2={{ill|Mogi Sadazumi|ja|茂木貞純}}|title=Shin Shinto Rituals and Ceremonial Guidebook|publisher=Eibun Kōshō Publishing|year=2011|pages=260–266}}</ref>
The term '''naorau''' (直らう), referring to receiving offerings such as rice cakes from a shrine, carries a similar meaning.
According to the guidelines of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}, naorai must be conducted in all Shinto rituals, with specific methods prescribed for its performance. However, some shrines follow traditional customs passed down through generations, and in such cases, those customs take precedence.
When seasonal vegetables, seafood, or other items are offered as shinsen, they are often cooked and served during naorai. Thus, at some shrines, the dishes served at naorai align with local cuisine.
Certain rituals involve priests dining with the deity ({{ill|Kenrei|ja|顕斎}}). While such rituals are distinct from naorai, they often use predetermined shinsen items.
== Etymology ==
The term "naorai" (なおらい) is generally understood to derive from "naori-ai" (直り合い), meaning "returning to normalcy together."<ref group="note">Motoori Norinaga explained, “Naorai is a contraction of ‘Naori-ai.’ The term ‘naori’ (straightening) signifies the act of releasing abstinence and returning to normalcy.” (From his commentary on the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}).</ref> It signifies the release of ritual abstinence and a return to daily life.
Alternatively, some scholars argue that its origin lies in the communal "naming and sharing" of food with the deity, as evidenced by terms like "namerai" in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} and "Naufai porridge" in the {{ill|Eke-shidai|ja|江家次第}}.<ref>{{cite|book|author=Jun'ichirō Ishikawa|contribution=Naorai|editor=Sakurai Tokutarō|title=Dictionary of Folk Beliefs|publisher=Tokyo-do Publishing|year=1980|pages=211–212}}</ref>
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} proposed that naorai is a ritual dedicated to the {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, conducted to correct any errors made during the preceding ceremony.
Another theory interprets "naoru" as "sitting down for a meal" and "au" as "gathering together," emphasizing the act of communal consumption of {{ill|Shinshu|ja|神酒}} and shinsen after the ritual, without the implication of 解戒 (release from abstinence).
== Procedure ==
According to the guidelines established in the "Shinto Rituals" by the Association of Shinto Shrines, the naorai process involves the following steps: 1. The ceremonial staff place the banquet trays before the participants. 2. The attendants pour sacred sake using a {{ill|Long-handled sake pourer|ja|長柄の銚子}}, dividing it into three pours. 3. Participants clap once, receive the sake, and begin eating the provided offerings.
The trays use {{ill|Oshiki|ja|折敷}}, with typical items including uncooked rice, cut squid, and cut kelp. Music may be performed during the ceremony but can be omitted.
== Notes == <references group="note" />

[[File:Votive offering of the Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|[[Shinsen]] ({{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}})|299x299px]] '''Naorai''' (直会) is a ritual conducted at the conclusion of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} ceremonies, during which participants drink {{ill|Shinshu (sacred sake)|ja|神酒}} and consume {{ill|Shinsen|ja|神饌}} as a form of communal dining and sharing (共飲共食 ritual).
== Overview ==
Although commonly seen as a celebratory banquet or after-party following a ceremony, naorai is an integral part of the Shinto ritual itself. By consuming the food and drink that were first offered to the deity, participants strengthen their connection with the divine spirit, receive its blessings, and anticipate its protection. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} interpreted it as a means of releasing oneself from ritual abstinence (解斎), but historical examples of naorai being performed during preparatory and principal rites suggest that interpreting it solely as 解斎 is a misunderstanding.<ref name="manual">{{cite|book|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2={{ill|Mogi Sadazumi|ja|茂木貞純}}|title=Shin Shinto Rituals and Ceremonial Guidebook|publisher=Eibun Kōshō Publishing|year=2011|pages=260–266}}</ref>
The term '''naorau''' (直らう), referring to receiving offerings such as rice cakes from a shrine, carries a similar meaning.
According to the guidelines of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}, naorai must be conducted in all Shinto rituals, with specific methods prescribed for its performance. However, some shrines follow traditional customs passed down through generations, and in such cases, those customs take precedence.
When seasonal vegetables, seafood, or other items are offered as shinsen, they are often cooked and served during naorai. Thus, at some shrines, the dishes served at naorai align with local cuisine.
Certain rituals involve priests dining with the deity ({{ill|Kenrei|ja|顕斎}}). While such rituals are distinct from naorai, they often use predetermined shinsen items.
== Etymology ==
The term "naorai" (なおらい) is generally understood to derive from "naori-ai" (直り合い), meaning "returning to normalcy together."<ref group="note">Motoori Norinaga explained, “Naorai is a contraction of ‘Naori-ai.’ The term ‘naori’ (straightening) signifies the act of releasing abstinence and returning to normalcy.” (From his commentary on the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}).</ref> It signifies the release of ritual abstinence and a return to daily life.
Alternatively, some scholars argue that its origin lies in the communal "naming and sharing" of food with the deity, as evidenced by terms like "namerai" in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} and "Naufai porridge" in the {{ill|Eke-shidai|ja|江家次第}}.<ref>{{cite|book|author=Jun'ichirō Ishikawa|contribution=Naorai|editor=Sakurai Tokutarō|title=Dictionary of Folk Beliefs|publisher=Tokyo-do Publishing|year=1980|pages=211–212}}</ref>
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} proposed that naorai is a ritual dedicated to the {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, conducted to correct any errors made during the preceding ceremony.
Another theory interprets "naoru" as "sitting down for a meal" and "au" as "gathering together," emphasizing the act of communal consumption of {{ill|Shinshu|ja|神酒}} and shinsen after the ritual, without the implication of 解戒 (release from abstinence).
== Procedure ==
According to the guidelines established in the "Shinto Rituals" by the Association of Shinto Shrines, the naorai process involves the following steps: 1. The ceremonial staff place the banquet trays before the participants. 2. The attendants pour sacred sake using a {{ill|Long-handled sake pourer|ja|長柄の銚子}}, dividing it into three pours. 3. Participants clap once, receive the sake, and begin eating the provided offerings.
The trays use {{ill|Oshiki|ja|折敷}}, with typical items including uncooked rice, cut squid, and cut kelp. Music may be performed during the ceremony but can be omitted.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
