== Overview ==
Although commonly seen as a celebratory banquet or after-party following a ceremony, naorai is an integral part of the Shinto ritual itself. By consuming the food and drink that were first offered to the deity, participants strengthen their connection with the divine spirit, receive its blessings, and anticipate its protection. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga}} interpreted it as a means of releasing oneself from ritual abstinence (解斎), but historical examples of naorai being performed during preparatory and principal rites suggest that interpreting it solely as 解斎 is a misunderstanding.<ref name="manual">{{cite|book|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2={{ill|Mogi Sadazumi|ja|茂木貞純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mogi Sadazumi|1=Sadasumi Motegi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21702496}}|title=Shin Shinto Rituals and Ceremonial Guidebook|publisher=Eibun Kōshō Publishing|year=2011|pages=260–266}}</ref>
The term '''naorau''' (直らう), referring to receiving offerings such as rice cakes from a shrine, carries a similar meaning.
According to the guidelines of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|Association of Shinto Shrines|WD=Q908077|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines}}, naorai must be conducted in all Shinto rituals, with specific methods prescribed for its performance. However, some shrines follow traditional customs passed down through generations, and in such cases, those customs take precedence.
When seasonal vegetables, seafood, or other items are offered as shinsen, they are often cooked and served during naorai. Thus, at some shrines, the dishes served at naorai align with local cuisine.
Certain rituals involve priests dining with the deity ({{ill|Kenrei|ja|顕斎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenrei|1=Kenrei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q86733907}}). While such rituals are distinct from naorai, they often use predetermined shinsen items.
== Etymology ==
The term "naorai" (なおらい) is generally understood to derive from "naori-ai" (直り合い), meaning "returning to normalcy together."<ref group="note">Motoori Norinaga explained, “Naorai is a contraction of ‘Naori-ai.’ The term ‘naori’ (straightening) signifies the act of releasing abstinence and returning to normalcy.” (From his commentary on the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}}).</ref> It signifies the release of ritual abstinence and a return to daily life.
Alternatively, some scholars argue that its origin lies in the communal "naming and sharing" of food with the deity, as evidenced by terms like "namerai" in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} and "Naufai porridge" in the {{ill|Eke-shidai|ja|江家次第|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eke-shidai|1=Eke-shidai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11550820}}.<ref>{{cite|book|author=Jun'ichirō Ishikawa|contribution=Naorai|editor=Sakurai Tokutarō|title=Dictionary of Folk Beliefs|publisher=Tokyo-do Publishing|year=1980|pages=211–212}}</ref>
{{ill|Shinobu Orikuchi|en|Shinobu Orikuchi|WD=Q3482286|lt=Orikuchi Shinobu}} proposed that naorai is a ritual dedicated to the {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}}, conducted to correct any errors made during the preceding ceremony.
Another theory interprets "naoru" as "sitting down for a meal" and "au" as "gathering together," emphasizing the act of communal consumption of {{ill|Omiki|en|Omiki|WD=Q3313722|lt=Shinshu}} and shinsen after the ritual, without the implication of 解戒 (release from abstinence).
== Procedure ==
According to the guidelines established in the "Shinto Rituals" by the Association of Shinto Shrines, the naorai process involves the following steps: 1. The ceremonial staff place the banquet trays before the participants. 2. The attendants pour sacred sake using a {{ill|Long-handled sake pourer|ja|長柄の銚子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Long-handled sake pourer|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 銚子|1=Chōshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2972145}}, dividing it into three pours. 3. Participants clap once, receive the sake, and begin eating the provided offerings.
The trays use {{ill|Oshiki|ja|折敷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神具|1=shingu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588759}}, with typical items including uncooked rice, cut squid, and cut kelp. Music may be performed during the ceremony but can be omitted.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kagami mochi|en|Kagami mochi|WD=Q1432988|lt=Kagami mochi}} - {{ill|Kagami biraki|en|Kagami biraki|WD=Q2030205|lt=Kagami biraki}} * {{ill|Sacramental bread|en|Sacramental bread|WD=Q207104|lt=Eucharist}} - {{ill|Eucharist|en|Eucharist|WD=Q66086|lt=Holy Communion}}

{{ill|Shinobu Orikuchi|en|Shinobu Orikuchi|WD=Q3482286|lt=Orikuchi Shinobu|1=Shinobu Orikuchi}} proposed that naorai is a ritual dedicated to the {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}}, conducted to correct any errors made during the preceding ceremony.

According to the guidelines established in the "Shinto Rituals" by the Association of Shinto Shrines, the naorai process involves the following steps: 1. The ceremonial staff place the banquet trays before the participants. 2. The attendants pour sacred sake using a {{ill|Long-handled sake pourer|ja|長柄の銚子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Long-handled sake pourer|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 銚子|1=Chōshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2972145}}, dividing it into three pours. 3. Alternatively, some scholars argue that its origin lies in the communal "naming and sharing" of food with the deity, as evidenced by terms like "namerai" in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} and "Naufai porridge" in the {{ill|Eke-shidai|ja|江家次第|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eke-shidai|1=Q11550820|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11550820}}.<ref>{{cite|book|author=Jun'ichirō Ishikawa|contribution=Naorai|editor=Sakurai Tokutarō|title=Dictionary of Folk Beliefs|publisher=Tokyo-do Publishing|year=1980|pages=211–212}}</ref>
{{ill|Shinobu Orikuchi|en|Shinobu Orikuchi|WD=Q3482286|lt=Orikuchi Shinobu|1=Shinobu Orikuchi}} proposed that naorai is a ritual dedicated to the {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}}, conducted to correct any errors made during the preceding ceremony.
Another theory interprets "naoru" as "sitting down for a meal" and "au" as "gathering together," emphasizing the act of communal consumption of {{ill|Omiki|en|Omiki|WD=Q3313722|lt=Shinshu|1=Omiki}} and shinsen after the ritual, without the implication of 解戒 (release from abstinence).
== Procedure ==
According to the guidelines established in the "Shinto Rituals" by the Association of Shinto Shrines, the naorai process involves the following steps: 1. The ceremonial staff place the banquet trays before the participants. 2. The attendants pour sacred sake using a {{ill|Long-handled sake pourer|ja|長柄の銚子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Long-handled sake pourer|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 銚子|1=Chōshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2972145}}, dividing it into three pours. 3. Participants clap once, receive the sake, and begin eating the provided offerings.
The trays use {{ill|Oshiki|ja|折敷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神具|1=Shingu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588759}}, with typical items including uncooked rice, cut squid, and cut kelp. Music may be performed during the ceremony but can be omitted.

revision.text:
{{Multiple issues|sources=August 28, 2017|original research=August 28, 2017}}
[[File:Votive offering of the Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|[[Shinsen]] ({{ill|Katori Shrine|en|Katori Shrine|WD=Q372380|lt=Katori Shrine|1=Katori Jingū}})|299x299px]] '''Naorai''' (直会) is a ritual conducted at the conclusion of {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}} ceremonies, during which participants drink {{ill|Omiki|en|Omiki|WD=Q3313722|lt=Shinshu (sacred sake)|1=Omiki}} and consume {{ill|Shinsen|en|Shinsen|WD=Q11591045|lt=Shinsen|1=Shinsen}} as a form of communal dining and sharing (共飲共食 ritual).
== Overview ==
Although commonly seen as a celebratory banquet or after-party following a ceremony, naorai is an integral part of the Shinto ritual itself. By consuming the food and drink that were first offered to the deity, participants strengthen their connection with the divine spirit, receive its blessings, and anticipate its protection. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} interpreted it as a means of releasing oneself from ritual abstinence (解斎), but historical examples of naorai being performed during preparatory and principal rites suggest that interpreting it solely as 解斎 is a misunderstanding.<ref name="manual">{{cite|book|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2={{ill|Mogi Sadazumi|ja|茂木貞純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mogi Sadazumi|1=Sadasumi Motegi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21702496}}|title=Shin Shinto Rituals and Ceremonial Guidebook|publisher=Eibun Kōshō Publishing|year=2011|pages=260–266}}</ref>
The term '''naorau''' (直らう), referring to receiving offerings such as rice cakes from a shrine, carries a similar meaning.
According to the guidelines of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|Association of Shinto Shrines|WD=Q908077|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}, naorai must be conducted in all Shinto rituals, with specific methods prescribed for its performance. However, some shrines follow traditional customs passed down through generations, and in such cases, those customs take precedence.
When seasonal vegetables, seafood, or other items are offered as shinsen, they are often cooked and served during naorai. Thus, at some shrines, the dishes served at naorai align with local cuisine.
Certain rituals involve priests dining with the deity ({{ill|Kenrei|ja|顕斎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenrei|1=Q86733907|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q86733907}}). While such rituals are distinct from naorai, they often use predetermined shinsen items.
== Etymology ==
The term "naorai" (なおらい) is generally understood to derive from "naori-ai" (直り合い), meaning "returning to normalcy together."<ref group="note">Motoori Norinaga explained, “Naorai is a contraction of ‘Naori-ai.’ The term ‘naori’ (straightening) signifies the act of releasing abstinence and returning to normalcy.” (From his commentary on the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi|1=Shoku Nihongi}}).</ref> It signifies the release of ritual abstinence and a return to daily life.
Alternatively, some scholars argue that its origin lies in the communal "naming and sharing" of food with the deity, as evidenced by terms like "namerai" in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} and "Naufai porridge" in the {{ill|Eke-shidai|ja|江家次第|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eke-shidai|1=Q11550820|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11550820}}.<ref>{{cite|book|author=Jun'ichirō Ishikawa|contribution=Naorai|editor=Sakurai Tokutarō|title=Dictionary of Folk Beliefs|publisher=Tokyo-do Publishing|year=1980|pages=211–212}}</ref>
{{ill|Shinobu Orikuchi|en|Shinobu Orikuchi|WD=Q3482286|lt=Orikuchi Shinobu|1=Shinobu Orikuchi}} proposed that naorai is a ritual dedicated to the {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}}, conducted to correct any errors made during the preceding ceremony.
Another theory interprets "naoru" as "sitting down for a meal" and "au" as "gathering together," emphasizing the act of communal consumption of {{ill|Omiki|en|Omiki|WD=Q3313722|lt=Shinshu|1=Omiki}} and shinsen after the ritual, without the implication of 解戒 (release from abstinence).
== Procedure ==
According to the guidelines established in the "Shinto Rituals" by the Association of Shinto Shrines, the naorai process involves the following steps: 1. The ceremonial staff place the banquet trays before the participants. 2. The attendants pour sacred sake using a {{ill|Long-handled sake pourer|ja|長柄の銚子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Long-handled sake pourer|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 銚子|1=Chōshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2972145}}, dividing it into three pours. 3. Participants clap once, receive the sake, and begin eating the provided offerings.
The trays use {{ill|Oshiki|ja|折敷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神具|1=Shingu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588759}}, with typical items including uncooked rice, cut squid, and cut kelp. Music may be performed during the ceremony but can be omitted.
